name: Santander-Besaya-Pas Valleys Loop
name_original: Santander, Liencres, Boo de Piélago, Alto de La Montaña, Los Corrales de Buelna, Santiurde de Toranzo – ruta circular
description: This difficult 66.6-mile road cycling route offers stunning coastal views and challenging climbs through Cantabria's diverse landscapes.
region_intro: Road cycling around Costera features diverse terrain, including river valleys, coastal marshes, and challenging mountain passes. The region is characterized by its varied elevation profiles, from routes along greenways to climbs through natural parks. Cyclists encounter landscapes shaped by rivers, estuaries, and hills, offering a mix of scenic coastal views and inland challenges. Best road cycling routes around Costera The most popular road cycling route is Liérganes, Río Miera, Portillo de la Sia, Asón, Alto de Alisas – circular through the Parque Natural de los Collados de Asón, a 59.9 miles (96.6 km) trail that takes 5 hours 42 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain through a natural park. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Alto del Caracol and Vía Verde del Pas Loop, a difficult 43.7 miles (70.3 km) path. This route combines a challenging climb with sections along a greenway, offering varied scenery. Local road cyclists also love the La Braguía and Pas Greenway Loop, a 58.8 miles (94.6 km) trail leading through river valleys and greenways, often completed in about 4 hours 31 minutes. Road cycling around Costera is defined by river valleys, coastal estuaries, and challenging mountain passes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to difficult climbs. The routes in Costera are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 500 reviews. More than 31,000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Costera's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect while road cycling in Costera?
A: Road cycling in Costera offers a diverse range of landscapes. You'll encounter scenic river valleys, coastal estuaries, and challenging mountain passes. The region is characterized by varied elevation profiles, from routes along greenways to climbs through natural parks, providing a mix of coastal views and inland challenges. You can also expect open landscapes and mountainous areas, particularly in regions like La Costera in Spain, which is known for its cyclotourism.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Costera, and what are their difficulty levels?
A: There are over 690 road cycling routes available in Costera. These routes cater to various skill levels: approximately 96 are easy, 371 are moderate, and 232 are difficult, offering options for every type of cyclist.

Q: Are there any family-friendly road cycling routes in Costera?
A: While many routes in Costera feature challenging climbs, there are options that incorporate greenways, which are often more suitable for families or those seeking an easier ride. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1005116328/alto-del-caracol-and-via-verde-del-pas-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto del Caracol and Vía Verde del Pas Loop&lt;/a&gt; combines a challenging climb with sections along a greenway, offering varied scenery that can be adapted for different family members. Look for routes that utilize the 'Vía Verde del Pas' for flatter, more accessible sections.

Q: What are some notable natural features or landmarks I might see along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes in Costera pass through significant natural areas. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1004554678/lierganes-rio-miera-portillo-de-la-sia-ason-alto-de-alisas-circular-through-the-parque-natural-de-los-collados-de-ason" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Liérganes, Río Miera, Portillo de la Sia, Asón, Alto de Alisas – circular through the Parque Natural de los Collados de Asón&lt;/a&gt; route takes you through the Parque Natural de los Collados de Asón. You might also encounter coastal estuaries like the Ría de Oriñón, as seen on the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1004578384/from-barco-de-viesca-to-castro-urdiales-round-trip-through-the-ria-de-orinon" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From Barco de Viesca to Castro Urdiales - round trip through the Ría de Oriñón&lt;/a&gt; route, or the Santoña Marshes, featured in the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1005118280/santona-marshes-and-collado-de-ason-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santoña Marshes and Collado de Asón Loop&lt;/a&gt;. These areas offer unique natural beauty and wildlife viewing opportunities.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in Costera?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes in Costera are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1004554678/lierganes-rio-miera-portillo-de-la-sia-ason-alto-de-alisas-circular-through-the-parque-natural-de-los-collados-de-ason" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Liérganes, Río Miera, Portillo de la Sia, Asón, Alto de Alisas – circular through the Parque Natural de los Collados de Asón&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1004556769/la-braguia-and-pas-greenway-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Braguía and Pas Greenway Loop&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1005115994/castro-urdiales-orinon-mercadillo-puerto-de-las-munecas-santullan-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro-Urdiales, Oriñón, Mercadillo, Puerto de Las Muñecas, Santullán – circular route&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Costera?
A: The road cycling routes in Costera are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 500 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, which includes challenging mountain passes and scenic greenways, as well as the beautiful river valleys and coastal views. The region's natural parks and well-maintained roads also contribute to a highly enjoyable cycling experience.

Q: Can I find routes that pass by waterfalls or lakes in Costera?
A: Yes, Costera offers routes where you can enjoy natural water features. While specific road cycling routes may not directly pass every waterfall or lake, the region has several notable attractions. You can find waterfalls like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5283013" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Suscuaja River Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; and a &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/8022536" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Waterfall and Scenic Trail&lt;/a&gt;. For lakes and marshlands, consider areas near &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3984180" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pozo Menor de Valcaba&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5410001" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santoña, Victoria and Joyel Marshes Natural Park&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any routes that offer coastal views?
A: Absolutely. The Costera region, particularly areas reminiscent of Spain's Costa Brava, is renowned for its stunning coastal cycling. Routes often provide extensive panoramic views of the Mediterranean Sea. While the guide data focuses on inland routes, the general region offers coastal stretches. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1005122794/laredo-ensenada-de-orinon-castro-urdiales-alto-de-la-mina-ampuero-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laredo, Ensenada de Oriñon, Castro-Urdiales, Alto de La Mina, Ampuero – circular route&lt;/a&gt; will bring you close to the coast and its views.

Q: What is the best time of year to go road cycling in Costera?
A: The region, particularly areas in Spain like La Costera, benefits from a pleasant climate that enhances outdoor activities. While specific seasonal conditions can vary, generally spring and autumn offer ideal temperatures for road cycling, avoiding the peak heat of summer and the colder, potentially wetter conditions of winter. Always check local weather forecasts before your ride.

Q: Are there any moderate difficulty routes for road cyclists looking for a good challenge without extreme climbs?
A: Yes, Costera offers a significant number of moderate routes, with 371 options available. These routes provide a good balance of distance and elevation without reaching the extreme difficulty of some mountain passes. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1005124978/las-arenillas-alto-de-la-mina-samano-return-via-castro-urdiales" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Las Arenillas, Alto de la Mina, Sámano – return via Castro Urdiales&lt;/a&gt;, which is a moderate 30.8-mile (49.5 km) route with a manageable elevation gain.

Q: Can I find routes that pass through historic towns or villages?
A: Yes, the region of La Costera in Spain is known for its historic towns. While specific routes in the guide data don't explicitly mention passing through Xàtiva, the general area offers opportunities to explore towns rich in history. Many routes will take you through charming villages, allowing you to experience the local culture and architecture.

Q: What are some of the longer, more challenging road cycling routes in Costera?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a significant challenge, Costera has several difficult routes with substantial distance and elevation gain. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1004554678/lierganes-rio-miera-portillo-de-la-sia-ason-alto-de-alisas-circular-through-the-parque-natural-de-los-collados-de-ason" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Liérganes, Río Miera, Portillo de la Sia, Asón, Alto de Alisas – circular through the Parque Natural de los Collados de Asón&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 59.9-mile (96.6 km) route with over 2,400 meters of elevation gain. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1005118280/santona-marshes-and-collado-de-ason-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santoña Marshes and Collado de Asón Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 102 km with nearly 2,000 meters of climbing.
tips: German-made locomotive that entered service in 1913 to transport iron ores and limestone from the Camargo mines to its factory. It is located on the greenway, near the train station, converted into an exhibition hall and tourist information office.

Also known as El Puente Rual, the aqueduct is next to the Vía Verde del Pas. It was built in 1885 in order to supply drinking water to the city of Santander. It is an ideal place to take a short break – there is shade, benches and tables. In front there is a bar and a fountain.
The Mountain through Las Presillas has a length of 3.16km, an altitude of 195 meters and an average slope of 4.5%. From here you can admire the beautiful hills and the great views.

Probably one of the nicest descents in the region. Approximately a 2 km long descent on a well asphalted road. Make sure you do it with good brakes because you will need them, it can get fast if you let it go. 

You can see the Pas river mouth into the sea between two tongues of sand. Very nice sunset if you do it in the afternoon. There is a balcony at the side of the road if you want to enjoy the views, but it is very tempting (in my opinion) to make the most out of your bike and just enjoy the speed.

It is really one of a kind descent where you can truly understand what Cantabria is; sea, beaches and mountains.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as difficult and requires good physical preparation. It features significant elevation gain and varied terrain, making it a challenging ride best suited for experienced road cyclists.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this circular route?
faqs.answer: The route offers a diverse mix of terrain. You'll primarily encounter paved surfaces, including asphalt and other paved roads, especially in the coastal sections around Santander, Liencres, and Boo de Piélago. However, as you venture inland towards Los Corrales de Buelna and Santiurde de Toranzo, there's a possibility of encountering unpaved or rougher sections, which can add to the challenge for a road bike.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: This route offers stunning and varied scenery, transitioning from the Cantabrian coastline to verdant inland valleys and mountainous areas. Key highlights include the Alto de La Montaña, which provides rewarding panoramic views, and the View of the Pas River Mouth. You'll also pass by historical landmarks such as the Reyerma Steam Locomotive and the Santiurde de Toranzo Aqueduct.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is generally during spring or early autumn. These seasons offer pleasant temperatures and less extreme weather, allowing you to fully enjoy the diverse landscapes of Cantabria, from its coastal views to its green valleys.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this circular route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking spots aren't detailed, you can typically find parking in the towns along the route, such as Santander, Liencres, or Boo de Piélago, which serve as good starting points for this circular journey.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this public road cycling route.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As this is a demanding road cycling route, it is generally not suitable for dogs to accompany you alongside the bike. If you wish to bring a dog, it would need to be securely carried in a specialized bike trailer or carrier, which might add to the challenge.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other paths. Notable sections include parts of the Vía Verde del Pas, Camino del Besaya: Cantabria, and the Northern Route (Cantabria).

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect on this ride?
faqs.answer: You can expect incredibly diverse and stunning scenery. The route transitions from the dramatic Cantabrian coastline with its sea, beaches, and cliffs, to the verdant inland areas featuring green valleys, rivers like the Besaya, and charming rural villages nestled in a mountainous backdrop. It truly showcases Cantabria's unique blend of marine and mountainous environments.

faqs.question: Are there places to stop for food or drink along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through several towns and villages such as Santander, Liencres, Los Corrales de Buelna, and Santiurde de Toranzo, you will find opportunities to stop for cafes, pubs, and other amenities to refuel and rest.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this challenging road cycling route?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and length, you should bring appropriate road cycling gear, including a well-maintained road bike, helmet, suitable cycling apparel, plenty of water and snacks, a repair kit, and navigation tools. Be prepared for varied terrain and potential unpaved sections.

waypoints.name: View of the Pas River Mouth
